frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
generated_comment: Savitri: Sunte ho? Sharmaji ki patni ने कल क्या कहा मुझसे — कि Pooja को उन्होंने आँगन में जींस में देखा। अब बाहर वाले देखते हैं, तो हमारे घर की, हमारे khandaan की बात होती है। Brahmin ghar ki bahu hai, kuch toh maryada hoti hai na? Savitri (continuing): Main samajhti hoon wo Delhi se aayi hai, wahan ka alag chalchalan hai — but yahan Varanasi mein, hamare mohalle mein, log dekhte hain. Andar kamre mein jo pehne pehne, humein kya — but aaangan mein, bahar nikle toh... thoda dhyaan rakhna chahiye. Kal unse baat karni chahiye — pyaar se, bura nahi manegi — ki beta, jo sanstha hum nibhaate hain, uski ek pehchaan hoti hai. Tum hamare ghar ki lakshmi ho, tumhare kapdon se bhi woh dikhna chahiye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment frames a woman's clothing choices as subject to community and family approval, positioning her body as a carrier of caste-family honour rather than as her own domain — a covert form of misogyny expressed through the language of tradition, belonging, and affectionate guidance rather than direct control or hostility.
persona_text: You are Savitri. Age 58. Married. A Brahmin woman in Varanasi. Your daughter in law Pooja who comes from a Brahmin family in Delhi has been living with you for one year. Pooja wears western clothes at home Ñ jeans, t-shirts, sometimes sleeveless tops. She wears a salwar kameez or saree when guests come or when there are functions. You have not said anything directly. But your neighbour made a comment last week about what she saw Pooja wearing in the courtyard and you have been thinking about it since. You are sitting with your husband tonight and telling him what the neighbour said and what you think needs to be said to Pooja about what is appropriate for a Brahmin daughter in law in a traditional household and what her clothes say about her respect for the family and the community.
